en.extract:
The long-tailed honey buzzard (Henicopernis longicauda) is a bird of prey in the family Accipitridae.
It is found in New Guinea and some neighboring island groups. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and subtropical or tropical moist montane forest.


Description
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is described as having a thin body, small head, long rounded tail and wide, round wings. They fly with their wrists forward while spreading their characteristically "fingered" primary feathers. The wing and tail feathers have dark black barring. The face and bill are pale and the iris is yellow. The head and breast feathers are lightly streaked. The total length, including the tail feathers, is reported to be from 50–61 cm (22 in). The wingspan ranges from 105–140 cm (48 in). The tail feather length ranges from 29–37 cm (13 in). The size of males as a proportion of the size of females was calculated to be 89%. Adults can be identified by their three visible boldly black bars on the body and wings, while the fourth bar is hidden. They have bluish-white feet. Juveniles can be identified by their much narrower bands, all four of which are visible.


Taxonomy
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is a member of the order Accipitriformes, and are considered typical diurnal raptors. They are found in the family Accipitridae, the hawks, eagles and allies. The Long-tailed honey buzzard is one of two species in the genus Henicopernis. The genus was found to be part of a clade inside of the pernine kites, along with Hamirostra and Lophoictinia. The four species of this new clade were the only accipitrids found to have a deletion (3 bp) within the RAG-1 sequence. This clade was found to be the sister clade to Aviceda, and a unification of its four species under the genus Hamirostra has been proposed. Counter to former reports, the genus was not found to be closely related to the genus Pernis.
The group common name "honey buzzard" for the genus Henicopernis is disagreed upon by some sources, as members of the genus Pernis are the "true honey buzzards" and members of the genus Henicopernis do not resemble true honey buzzards any more than they resemble Square-tailed and Black-breasted kites, their more closely related sister taxa. Therefore, the common name Long-tailed buzzard is also frequently used for this species.


Habitat and distribution
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is native to New Guinea and its surrounding islands. These include the Bay Islands of Japen and Biak, the Northwest Islands of Misool, Batanta, Salawati and Waigeo, the Aru Islands, the Southeast Islands of Fergusson and Normanby. On mainland New Guinea, they are reported to be found in the Trans-Fly savannah ecoregion on the southern end of the island, but also more broadly throughout New Guinea, except for the mountainous regions of the Western, Central and Eastern ranges.
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is found typically in the regions’ lowland rainforests, hill forests and montane forests. It is a rainforest specialist and is restricted to New Guinea's rainforest habitats. It is found up to elevations of 3000 m. Long-tailed honey buzzards do not migrate and are considered endemic to New Guinea. There are estimated to be at most 100,000 individuals of this species.


Behavior
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is described as the quintessential hawk seen flying overhead in the forests of the region. They are observed to prefer remaining alone or in pairs. They sail and circle slowly above the canopy cover of the forest, but also pass through open regions below the canopy where they perch to observe their surroundings. They are reported to not be shy and are easily spotted. They are diurnal (active during the day) and crepuscular (active around twilight).


Diet
The Long-tailed honey buzzard feeds primarily on insects such as ants, grasshoppers and especially adult wasps and wasp larvae. They follow wasps to their nests and pull out larvae with their feet. They also feed on bird and lizard eggs, tree-climbing lizards, small mammals and small birds. One sighting recounts seeing an individual catching land-bound prey in a grassland near a forest and carrying it back into the forest to consume.


Vocalizations
The only published information on Long-tailed honey buzzard vocalizations describes the series of calls they produce during display as “goshawk-like”. Typical goshawk display calls include many rapid notes in succession, called a chatter, or a single long call, referred to as a wail. Otherwise, they are described as being usually silent.


Reproduction
Long-tailed honey buzzards typically build stick nests in the crowns of tall trees. They may have preferences for Auracarias, Planchonellas and Pandanus. They may also build their nests as low as only 7 m, or on the edges of cliffs. Their breeding season takes place from late in the wet season through to the dry season, which occurs from New Guinea’s autumn through to the spring. No egg information is currently known, including clutch size. One observation record observed a single offspring.


Conservation threats
The most pressing threat to the Long-tailed honey buzzard is deforestation. Although considered of least concern status, population numbers, trends and biology are poorly researched, and populations in some regions may be declining significantly.


References


External links

BirdLife species factsheet for Long-tailed honey-buzzard
Henicopernis longicauda, species account at Global Raptor Information Network

The '''long-tailed honey buzzard''' ('''''Henicopernis longicauda''''') is a [[bird of prey]] in the family [[Accipitridae]].

It is found in [[New Guinea]] and some neighboring island groups.<ref>[[Raja Ampat Islands|Raja Ampat]], [[Aru Islands|Aru]], [[Biak Islands|Biak]], and [[Yapen]] groups. {{cite web |last1=Legre |first1=Leo |date=2010 |title=Henicopernis longicauda: additional details on distribution |url=http://www.globalraptors.org/grin/SpeciesExtended.asp?specID=8282&catID=2000 |access-date=15 May 2016 |website=Global Raptor Information Network |publisher=The Peregrine Fund}}</ref> Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]].
== Description ==
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is described as having a thin body, small head, long rounded tail and wide, round wings.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last1=Pratt |first1=Thane K. |title=Birds of New Guinea |last2=Beehler |first2=Bruce McP |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0-691-09562-2 |edition=Second |series=Princeton field guides |location=Princeton}}</ref> They fly with their wrists forward while spreading their characteristically "fingered" primary feathers.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Ferguson-Lees |first=James |title=Raptors of the world |date=2001 |publisher=Christopher Helm |others=David A. Christie, Kim Franklin, David Mead, Philip Burton |isbn=978-1-4081-3550-1 |series=Helm identification guides |location=London}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Citation |title=Accipitriformes |date=2016-03-29 |work=Birds of New Guinea: Distribution, Taxonomy, and Systematics |pages=198–213 |url=https://www.degruyterbrill.com/document/doi/10.1515/9781400880713-018/html |access-date=2025-11-04 |publisher=Princeton University Press |language=en |doi=10.1515/9781400880713-018 |isbn=978-1-4008-8071-3|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The wing and tail feathers have dark black barring.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> The face and bill are pale<ref name=":0" /> and the iris is yellow.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> The head and breast feathers are lightly streaked<ref name=":0" />'''.''' The total length, including the tail feathers, is reported to be from 50–61 cm (22 in).<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> The wingspan ranges from 105–140 cm (48 in).<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> The tail feather length ranges from 29–37 cm (13 in).<ref name=":2" /> The size of males as a proportion of the size of females was calculated to be 89%.<ref name=":2" /> Adults can be identified by their three visible boldly black bars on the body and wings, while the fourth bar is hidden.<ref name=":0" /> They have bluish-white feet.<ref name=":2" /> Juveniles can be identified by their much narrower bands, all four of which are visible.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

== Taxonomy ==
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is a member of the order Accipitriformes, and are considered typical diurnal raptors.<ref name=":1" /> They are found in the family Accipitridae, the hawks, eagles and allies.<ref name=":1" /> The Long-tailed honey buzzard is one of two species in the genus ''Henicopernis''.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /> The genus was found to be part of a [[clade]] inside of the [[Perninae|pernine kites]], along with ''[[Black-breasted buzzard|Hamirostra]]'' and ''[[Square-tailed kite|Lophoictinia]]''.<ref name=":3" /> The four species of this new clade were the only accipitrids found to have a deletion (3 bp) within the RAG-1 sequence.<ref name=":3" /> This clade was found to be the sister clade to ''[[Aviceda]],'' and a unification of its four species under the genus ''Hamirostra'' has been proposed.<ref name=":3" /> Counter to former reports, the genus was not found to be closely related to the genus ''[[Pernis (bird)|Pernis]]''.<ref name=":3" />

The group common name "honey buzzard" for the genus ''Henicopernis'' is disagreed upon by some sources, as members of the genus ''Pernis'' are the "true honey buzzards" and members of the genus ''Henicopernis'' do not resemble true honey buzzards any more than they resemble Square-tailed and Black-breasted kites, their more closely related sister taxa.<ref name=":1" /> Therefore, the common name Long-tailed buzzard is also frequently used for this species.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

== Habitat and distribution ==
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is native to New Guinea and its surrounding islands.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> These include the Bay Islands of Japen and Biak, the Northwest Islands of Misool, Batanta, Salawati and Waigeo, the Aru Islands, the Southeast Islands of Fergusson and Normanby.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> On mainland New Guinea, they are reported to be found in the Trans-Fly savannah ecoregion on the southern end of the island,<ref name=":1" /> but also more broadly throughout New Guinea, except for the mountainous regions of the Western, Central and Eastern ranges.<ref name=":2" />

The Long-tailed honey buzzard is found typically in the regions’ lowland rainforests, hill forests and montane forests.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> It is a rainforest specialist and is restricted to New Guinea's rainforest habitats.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Gamauf |first=A. |last2=Haring |first2=E. |date=2004 |title=Molecular phylogeny and biogeography of Honey-buzzards (genera Pernis and Henicopernis) |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1439-0469.2004.00250.x |journal=Journal of Zoological Systematics and Evolutionary Research |language=en |volume=42 |issue=2 |pages=145–153 |doi=10.1111/j.1439-0469.2004.00250.x |issn=1439-0469|url-access=subscription }}</ref> It is found up to elevations of 3000 m.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> Long-tailed honey buzzards do not migrate and are considered [[Endemism|endemic]] to New Guinea.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Barrowclough |first=George F. |last2=Groth |first2=Jeff G. |last3=Lai |first3=Jonas E. |last4=Tsang |first4=Susan M. |date=March 2014 |title=The Phylogenetic Relationships of the Endemic Genera of Australo-Papuan Hawks |url=http://www.bioone.org/doi/abs/10.3356/JRR-13-33.1 |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |language=en |volume=48 |issue=1 |pages=36–43 |doi=10.3356/JRR-13-33.1 |issn=0892-1016}}</ref> There are estimated to be at most 100,000 individuals of this species.<ref name=":2" />

== Behavior ==
The Long-tailed honey buzzard is described as the quintessential hawk seen flying overhead in the forests of the region.<ref name=":0" /> They are observed to prefer remaining alone or in pairs.<ref name=":0" /> They sail and circle slowly above the canopy cover of the forest, but also pass through open regions below the canopy where they perch to observe their surroundings.<ref name=":0" /> They are reported to not be shy and are easily spotted.<ref name=":0" /> They are [[Diurnality|diurnal]] (active during the day) and [[Crepuscular animal|crepuscular]] (active around twilight).<ref name=":2" />

=== Diet ===
The Long-tailed honey buzzard feeds primarily on insects such as ants, grasshoppers and especially adult wasps and wasp larvae.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last=Debus |first=Stephen |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |last3=Marks |first3=Jeffrey S. |date=2020 |title=Long-tailed Honey-buzzard (Henicopernis longicauda), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/lthbuz1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.lthbuz1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> They follow wasps to their nests and pull out larvae with their feet.<ref name=":4" /> They also feed on bird and lizard eggs, tree-climbing lizards, small mammals and small birds.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> One sighting recounts seeing an individual catching land-bound prey in a grassland near a forest and carrying it back into the forest to consume.<ref name=":4" />

=== Vocalizations ===
The only published information on Long-tailed honey buzzard vocalizations describes the series of calls they produce during display as “goshawk-like”.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> Typical goshawk display calls include many rapid notes in succession, called a chatter, or a single long call, referred to as a wail.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mlodinow |first=Steven G. |date=2024 |title=Eurasian Goshawk (Astur gentilis), version 1.1 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/norgos1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.norgos1.01.1 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Otherwise, they are described as being usually silent.<ref name=":0" />

=== Reproduction ===
Long-tailed honey buzzards typically build stick nests in the crowns of tall trees.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> They may have preferences for [[Araucaria|Auracarias]], [[Planchonella]]s and [[Pandanus]].<ref name=":4" /> They may also build their nests as low as only 7 m, or on the edges of cliffs.<ref name=":4" /> Their breeding season takes place from late in the wet season through to the dry season, which occurs from New Guinea’s autumn through to the spring.<ref name=":4" /> No egg information is currently known, including clutch size.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> One observation record observed a single offspring.<ref name=":4" />

== Conservation threats ==
The most pressing threat to the Long-tailed honey buzzard is deforestation.<ref name=":4" /> Although considered of least concern status, population numbers, trends and biology are poorly researched, and populations in some regions may be declining significantly.<ref name=":4" />
